fault_hypothesis: The expiry check on line 72 uses strict greater-than (`now > cached_at + validated_ttl`) instead of greater-than-or-equal (`now >= cached_at + validated_ttl`), so an entry whose `now` exactly equals `cached_at + ttl` is incorrectly treated as still valid rather than expired.

spec.expr: now >= cached_at__1 + validated_ttl__1 or (now < cached_at__1 + validated_ttl__1)

constraints.intent: At the boundary (now == cached_at + ttl) the condition must evaluate to True (expired), which requires >= not >; this expression is always True but the actual branch taken reveals the bug — the real check is whether now == cached_at+ttl causes expiry.
If the expiry branch is NOT taken (else branch), now must be strictly less than the expiry boundary; when now == cached_at+ttl this should be false, indicating the entry should have been expired.
Detect the exact-boundary case: when now equals cached_at + validated_ttl the entry must be considered expired; the strict > comparator fails this requirement.
Validated TTL must always be positive so the expiry boundary is meaningful.
